chapters.title: American law is overdue for an overhaul
chapters.text:
Over the past few decades, American society has changed faster than ever before, growing and maturing along with the rest of the world. That is why the US Constitution, once a symbol of democracy and progress, now needs urgent revision. Since it was written, the Constitution has mainly protected white male power, with modern political freedoms bolted on later.

The point is that it is an old document, out of step with the diverse population of the United States today.

Many ideas, drafted and revised in a single summer, still let some people defend their prejudices. Remember the case of a gay couple that could not buy a cake from a homophobic baker. We will get back to that story later.

On top of that, minority groups had little say in the original text, or in the amendments added later to protect their rights. Since women and people of color rarely held important seats in Congress or on the Supreme Court, their views were largely missing from the document. As a result, the Constitution mainly reflects the outlook and interests of one majority while overlooking the voices and concerns of many marginalized groups affected by its rules.

No document or law is perfect or beyond criticism. As society changes, our legal frameworks should change with it.

Much of the problem is also about how the law is read. Drawing on his legal background, Elie Mystal offers a different perspective, one the Constitution has overlooked and pushed to the side. Seeing that angle may push people to demand change at last.

Equality and fairness have to meet the standards of our modern definitions of those ideals, or the whole American experiment is illegitimate.

So, let us peel back the confusing layers of legal jargon to see what is wrong with conservative readings and how to give the Constitution the fresh start it badly needs.

chapters.title: Free speech is often misunderstood
chapters.text:
Before we get into the details, let us talk about canceling. One of the earliest examples goes back to ancient Greece, where Socrates was sentenced to death for opposing democracy. He thought only educated and qualified people should have a voice, and he faced legal consequences for saying so.

So, let us try to put cancel culture in context. People with unpopular views can speak their minds. The Constitution gives them that right. But it cannot protect a person's book sales or social media account when their words and ideas backfire.

Free speech lets you say what you want, but it does not shield you from the fallout of your words.

Still, there are bigger threats to free expression than the fuss over cancel culture. Conservatives, for example, often take legal action against independent journalists to manage their public image, which does real damage to free speech.

Another harmful pattern is the weaponization of free speech, which hits the LGBTQ community, people of color, women, and religious minorities. A clear example is the firing of Native Americans and the denial of unemployment benefits, all because of their use of a ceremonial hallucinogen that was wrongly labeled as drug use. That ruling paved the way for the Religious Freedom Restoration Act, or RFRA, which was meant to give a single reading of religious freedom. While it was supposed to protect the right to practice religion, the RFRA can be used to push Judeo Christian ideas onto secular matters. The Court, for instance, ruled that Hobby Lobby stores can refuse to provide birth control to female employees, in effect denying them basic healthcare.

The misuse of the RFRA does not stop at the corporate level. Remember the cake story. In 2012, Charlie and David, a same sex couple, married in Massachusetts, since same sex marriage was not yet legal in their home state of Colorado. They wanted to celebrate at home and asked Masterpiece Cakeshop to make a cake. The owner, Jack Philips, refused because of his religious beliefs.

It is easy to say, just go to a different bakery, but think about the casual discrimination minorities deal with every day. Baking a cake for a gay couple does not go against Jack Philips's religious beliefs, and no one forced him to do anything against his faith. This case is a small example of how prejudiced people twist free speech to keep discrimination alive.

We should defend the principles behind the law, not the exact text and technicalities.

chapters.title: Loopholes that drive police brutality
chapters.text:
Republican readings of the law can raise concerns, but the exploitation of loopholes by law enforcement is far more frightening, especially for Black people. Elie Mystal remembers an incident when police pulled him over on a highway. He was not arrested, but what happened next was worse, a Terry stop. Unlike an arrest, a Terry stop does not require probable cause and does not give the rights protected by the Fourth Amendment.

The police held him face down on the ground while they searched his car, never asking for his license and registration. He was lucky to walk away unharmed. Many Black people are not so lucky.

The Terry stop grew out of the Terry v. Ohio case. Its original goal was to let officers question suspects without enough cause for an arrest. It quickly turned into a tool for racial profiling. A Terry stop only needs reasonable suspicion that someone committed or is about to commit a crime. That loose standard lets officers act on their biases. They can stop a Black man waiting for his friend next to a store on the assumption that he is about to rob it.

Some have tried to end the practice with class action lawsuits to remind people that racial profiling is unconstitutional. Those efforts have not done much for the average Black teen driving alone on an Indiana highway.

Take the 1989 case of Graham v. Connor. Graham was the target of police brutality after acting suspiciously at a store. He had simply driven off after seeing a long line. In his lawsuit against the use of force, the original claim focused on discrimination, which the Fourteenth Amendment bans. The Court, however, brought up the Fourth Amendment and ruled that the officer's search was reasonable.

The Court decided that the police should be the ones to judge whether the force was reasonable, which made police brutality easier. Fixing this problem falls to state and local governments, which is hard even in liberal states.

Prosecuting the violent treatment of unarmed people by law enforcement can help stop police brutality.

Eventually, a bill passed that swapped the wording from reasonable to necessary when describing the use of force. The case shows a clear misunderstanding of how to address brutality. Even with that change, the officer who shot an unarmed Black teenager still controls the story. Letting only the aggressor decide whether an innocent person should live lets the police keep committing violence with no real consequences.

chapters.title: Human rights are hard to defend for minorities
chapters.text:
To understand the issue more deeply, let us go back to the 19th century, when Black people could vote only if they had lived in a state for three years and owned at least $250 worth of property. At the time, very few people were willing to sell land to minorities. When John and Elizabeth Whitehead divided and sold their Manhattan farmland, Black people bought half the lots, mostly to gain voting rights.

The land was later taken to build Central Park, since the property was declared subject to eminent domain. So what is eminent domain. It is the power of the government to take private property for public use, usually with compensation to the owner. It is worth noting that all land is, in a legal sense, public, and the idea of private ownership is a newer concept that still gets debated.

In short, if you live on land subject to eminent domain, the government can take it when it sees fit, with the promise of just compensation, a phrase with no fixed meaning. That looseness lets those in power, mostly white people, take wealth away from Black people.

The Black residents of Seneca Village, a settlement in Manhattan that was declared eminent domain, tried to sue the city and lost every time. That raised another issue, the jury trial. While the Constitution guarantees the right to a trial by an impartial jury, it is fair to ask whether juries really deserve our full trust. Juries, picked at random, can sometimes act in cruel ways and pay little attention during the trial.

It is also worth noting that a jury of 12 white people may not be much different from a single white judge hearing a case that involves Black people. The justice system can quietly keep Black jurors out by letting lawyers question and reject them without giving a reason.

It is important to recognize that an all white jury may not be truly impartial when a Black person is on trial.

The Batson v. Kentucky case tried to address this problem. James Batson was charged with burglary on thin evidence, and the jury had only one Black member. At a retrial, the prosecutor rejected every potential Black juror. Seeing the unfairness, public defender David Niehaus asked for the jury to be dismissed under the Fourteenth Amendment.

Today, the Batson challenge lets the defendant push back on the removal of Black jurors and ask for a race neutral reason for their exclusion. Lawyers still come up with weak excuses like body language, but it is still a step in the right direction.

chapters.title: How the Constitution still shapes minority life
chapters.text:
None of the issues covered so far is the biggest one. From the start, enslaved people were counted as three fifths of a free person, a rule meant to help slave states outnumber the free states of the North.

The Constitution was full of racist loopholes from the beginning. Trying to amend it now is, therefore, a tough and complex job. South Africa, in contrast, threw out its old law and started over. The new 1996 South African Constitution is now seen as a global model.

Some argue that the Bill of Rights, the first ten amendments, was not a real fix. It only treated a Black citizen as a whole person. The Constitution still hands power to white men, and they are slow to give it up. Even if we cannot change prejudiced minds, we can push for laws that do not protect their racist and sexist views.

Although the amendments are meant to safeguard minority rights, some white people refuse to accept that reality. Still, that is the only way to end the white supremacy that is built into the Constitution.

Beyond politics, the Fourteenth Amendment is supposed to enforce economic equality, but the Supreme Court has narrowed that power. It could be a strong tool to stop white owned monopolies from crushing small Black businesses. The problem is that, in the 19th century, white people had no duty to hire or pay Black people equally, which deepened segregation.

Social and economic equality matter as much as political equality.

Civil equality was not the goal of the Fourteenth Amendment. If the white men who backed it had known it would let their daughters marry a Black man, they would never have approved it. We badly need to rethink the whole idea of the amendments instead of clinging to the text alone.

Our founders were more Lannister than Stark, a group of rich people rebelling against a mad king, who were not really interested in freeing anyone but themselves.

Although it is now clear that the Fourteenth Amendment was not meant to protect interracial marriage, the legal scholar Steven Calabresi argues that the wording could be read, on a technical level, to allow such marriages, based on the dictionary meanings of the key terms.

chapters.title: Time to take the female view seriously
chapters.text:
The Constitution is not kind to women either. It does not, for example, guarantee the right to abortion, privacy, or contraception. Unsurprisingly, the lawmakers of the time, mostly white men, thought husbands could beat their wives.

Marital rape was not even recognized as a crime until the 1970s. That alone shows how harshly the original laws treated women. Given how white men, both past and present, have ignored the rights of children and women, it is hard to believe they truly care about the rights of a fetus in the abortion debate. That is why the conversation about the constitutional right to abortion should start with the basic idea that women are people, and so they deserve privacy and full control over their own bodies.

Let us go back to 1873. Anthony Comstock, a Christian reformer who believed abstinence was the only acceptable form of contraception, pushed for a law that banned shipping contraceptives across state lines. Those rules were loosened over time, but the approval of the birth control pill in 1960 finally gave women a real reproductive choice.

In response to that new kind of equality, the patriarchy began attacking reproductive clinics that provided such medicine to married women. The heated debates of the 1960s and 1970s eventually led to the landmark Roe v. Wade case. That case recognized abortion rights under the right to privacy protected by the Fourteenth Amendment. The Court also said, though, that the state had an interest in protecting potential life, which allowed limits on abortion. The case was overturned in 2022, with the claim that abortion is not a constitutional right.

Women should not be forced to give birth, just as no one should be forced into labor.

State interest often overrides the rights of protected groups and allows discrimination. Even when the Court recognizes a right to abortion, restrictions can make it hard for women to get care in time. Before the procedure, women often have to go through informed consent, which can mean someone makes them feel guilty about the decision, a 24 hour waiting period to extend that guilt, parental consent for minors that makes teen pregnancies harder to manage, and notification of the husband, which shifts the decision to the man.

Ideas like these, written into the Constitution, show how minorities suffer from outdated rules. Americans should focus on making a real change.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
A conservative Supreme Court can ignore or rewrite any law or principle, which makes the Court a kind of test for progress. Congress has the power to enforce the amendments, but the Court often decides how they are applied.

Unlike the United States, many other countries do not give their courts the same power to strike down laws. Limiting the authority of the Supreme Court may be necessary, but it has its own costs. In some cases, it is important to overrule vague conservative laws to keep discrimination from taking hold.

Fixing the situation does not mean tearing down the whole judicial branch. It means making the system reflect the country's diverse population. That change includes limiting federal judges' terms through a senior status designation, where they can take cases and get paid while giving up their federal bench seat. This approach may push the courts to be more responsive to election results and keep hard right extremists from shaping an entire generation's view of human rights.

The Constitution is a tougher problem. Given how a 250 year old document treats minorities, the best way to undo the harm may be to start over, the way South Africa did. We would need a representative body to write it, one that includes women, people of color, and other minorities this time. Everyone deserves fair treatment, and big changes are needed to make that real.

Try this

Do not accept the conservative reading of the law. You can fight political and social injustice by getting involved.

Listen to minorities and learn about their history so you understand their experience.

Read a book by a minority author to see their struggles up close.

Talk to your representatives and demand justice.

Read it till the end?
